stem: In the ring \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \), with \( [[nval]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \cdot [[q]]^{[[b]]} \), an element is a UNIT exactly when it is coprime to \( [[nval]] \). How many units does \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \) have?
solution: The units of \( \mathbb{Z}_{n} \) are exactly the residues coprime to \( n \), and there are \( \varphi(n) \) of them (Euler's totient). Here \( \varphi([[nval]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats every nonzero residue as a unit, i.e. treats \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \) as a field; but \( [[nval]] \) is composite, so some nonzero residues are zero divisors, not units.
This counts every residue including \( 0 \), which is never a unit.
This uses the totient formula for the SQUAREFREE number \( pq \), dropping the prime-power exponents \( [[a]] \) and \( [[b]] \).
This guesses that exactly half the residues are units, which is only a coincidence for some moduli, not a general rule.
This is one more than the correct unit count.
This is one fewer than the correct unit count.
This is two more than the correct unit count.

stem: Let \( n = [[nval]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \), a power of the single prime \( [[p]] \). The ring \( \mathbb{Z}_{n} \) is NOT an integral domain (for \( [[a]] > 1 \), any multiple of \( [[p]] \) is a zero divisor). How many units does \( \mathbb{Z}_{n} \) have?
solution: A residue mod \( p^{a} \) is a unit exactly when it is not a multiple of \( p \). There are \( n = [[nval]] \) residues total and \( n/p = [[nval]]/[[p]] \) multiples of \( p \) among them, leaving \( \varphi(p^{a}) = p^{a-1}(p-1) = [[answer]] \) units.
distractor_rationales: This treats every nonzero residue as a unit, ignoring that every multiple of \( [[p]] \) below \( [[nval]] \) is a zero divisor, not a unit.
This counts every residue including \( 0 \), which is never a unit.
This uses the totient of the prime \( [[p]] \) itself, forgetting the extra factor \( p^{a-1} \) that appears for a prime POWER modulus.
This is the count of multiples of \( [[p]] \) (the zero divisors and 0), the complement of the units, not the units themselves.
This is one more than the correct unit count.
This is one fewer than the correct unit count.
This overshoots the correct unit count by \( [[p]] \).

stem: In \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \), every nonzero element is either a UNIT or a ZERO DIVISOR (this dichotomy is exactly why \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) fails to be an integral domain unless \( [[n]] \) is prime). How many of the \( [[n]]-1 \) nonzero elements of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) are zero divisors (i.e. NOT units)?
solution: Of the \( [[n]] \) residues mod \( [[n]] \), exactly \( \varphi([[n]]) \) are units and one is \( 0 \); every other nonzero residue is a zero divisor. So the zero-divisor count is \( ([[n]]-1) - \varphi([[n]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the number of UNITS \( \varphi([[n]]) \), the complement of what was asked.
This still includes \( 0 \) in the non-unit count; \( 0 \) is excluded since the question asks for NONZERO zero divisors.
This claims every nonzero element is a zero divisor, as if \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) had no units at all.
This is one less than the unit count \( \varphi([[n]]) \), not the zero-divisor count.
This is one more than the correct zero-divisor count.
This is one fewer than the correct zero-divisor count.
This is two more than the correct zero-divisor count.

stem: In \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \), the element \( x = [[x]] \) is a unit if and only if \( \gcd(x, n) = 1 \). Compute \( 1 \) if \( [[x]] \) is a unit in \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \), or \( 0 \) if it is a zero divisor.
solution: Since \( \gcd([[x]], [[n]]) \) determines invertibility mod \( [[n]] \), \( [[x]] \) is a unit exactly when this gcd equals \( 1 \); computing it directly gives the verdict \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the flipped verdict, swapping unit and zero-divisor.
This is not a valid 0/1 verdict at all — the answer must be exactly 0 or 1.
This is not a valid 0/1 verdict at all — the answer must be exactly 0 or 1.
This is not a valid 0/1 verdict at all — the answer must be exactly 0 or 1.
This is not a valid 0/1 verdict at all — the answer must be exactly 0 or 1.
This reports the gcd value itself rather than the 0/1 unit verdict.

stem: The product ring \( R = \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \times \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) is NEVER an integral domain, even when both factors are fields, because \( (1,0)\cdot(0,1) = (0,0) \) exhibits zero divisors. An element \( (x,y) \) of \( R \) is a unit exactly when \( x \) is a unit of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \) AND \( y \) is a unit of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \). How many units does \( R \) have?
solution: Units of a direct product are pairs of units, one from each factor: there are \( \varphi([[m]]) \) unit choices for \( x \) and \( \varphi([[n]]) \) for \( y \), so \( |R^{\times}| = \varphi([[m]])\varphi([[n]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This applies Euler's totient to the PRODUCT \( mn \) directly, which is only valid when \( \gcd(m,n)=1 \); in general \( \varphi(mn) \ne \varphi(m)\varphi(n) \).
This adds the two per-factor unit counts instead of multiplying them; units of a product multiply, they don't add.
This is the full size \( |R| = mn \), not the count of units.
This is one less than the full size \( |R| \), as if only the single pair \( (0,0) \) failed to be a unit.
This is one more than the correct unit count.
This is one fewer than the correct unit count.
This is two more than the correct unit count.
